howItWorks.intro: This early retirement calculator uses standard formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: FIRE Number = Annual Expenses / Withdrawal Rate
Years = Time for savings + growth to reach FIRE Number
howItWorks.outro: Tax laws and financial markets change frequently. Verify current rates with your financial institution.

faq.question: What is the FIRE number?
faq.answer: Your FIRE (Financial Independence, Retire Early) number is annual expenses divided by your safe withdrawal rate. At 4% withdrawal, you need 25× your annual expenses.

faq.question: What is the 4% rule?
faq.answer: The Trinity Study found that withdrawing 4% of a diversified portfolio annually (adjusted for inflation) has a very high success rate over 30 years. Some prefer 3.5% for extra safety.

faq.question: What about healthcare before 65?
faq.answer: Early retirees need private health insurance until Medicare at 65. Budget $500-$1,500/month per person via ACA marketplace plans.

faq.question: Can I really retire in my 30s or 40s?
faq.answer: Yes, if your savings rate is high enough. At a 50% savings rate, you can retire in about 17 years. At 70% savings rate, about 8.5 years. The math works.

faq.question: Is the Early Retirement Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

sections.title: What Is the Early Retirement Calculator?
sections.body: The Early Retirement Calculator is a free online tool designed for individuals and families who need quick, accurate calculations in the financial planning space. By entering your annual expenses, current savings, monthly savings, you get instant results including fire number, gap to close, years to fire. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting fire number right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In financial planning, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by individuals and families worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to manage your finances with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your annual expenses and need to find the right fire number. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Annual Expenses ($)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your annual expenses — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: current savings, monthly savings, return rate, withdrawal rate. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your fire number, gap to close, years to fire. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your annual expenses before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both fire number and gap to close — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to manage your finances.
If you're unsure about your withdrawal rate, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
